Speaker 1: In this video I'm gonna be reviewing finding your location, and choosing the perfect
venue to host your event. Whether it's a small group, a workshop, a mastermind, or

The first thing on your list when looking for a venue to host your event is just gonna be a

on here that are my favorite.
The first one is gonna be called EVENTup.com. This is just a spot that you can go to, it

by the location that you're choosing to host the event. It'll give you resources to search
the number of guests that you wanna host, whether you want seated or standing, your

then it's up to you to just pick and choose your favorites, and then go ahead and
research those places and contact them for extra details.
Another one that's a really great resource is Cvent. This is gonna give you options for a
larger scale venue. So if you're looking to host an event that is a little bit more, and you
need a room that's bigger, or if you just wanna check out a resort or a hotel that can
host a couple more sleeping rooms, this is a great option to look at. It's gonna give you
the details of how many sleeping rooms the hotel has, what their largest meeting room
space is, and also what the total meeting space is on the property. And this is just gonna
list out all of those options. It's a great place to go to, and from there you can take your
top favorites, and really go and contact them.
Keep in mind as you are searching for your venue, that the location is really gonna be
the first impression that your attendees experience. So if you have a personal brand, or
a style that you want to be conscious of, just make sure that that really serves the needs
of your attendees. And if you need to provide additional things like what options are in
the area that is really gonna serve that niche, it may help fill the room a little bit better
as well.
Space for the purpose of the event is really the most important thing you're gonna be
looking at when you're looking at the venue. Whether it's hosting a five to ten person

where all of the magic happens. And we wanna make sure that there's room in there for
everyone to feel comfortable but feel included, but also give you enough space to really
facilitate what you're planning to do.
Accomplishing all of those items includes making sure that you have room at the front
for your speakers, all the guest speakers, and then your expert. Make sure that they're

board, make sure that there's items in there for the things that you're really gonna need

few tables for project items, any handouts, and staff that are gonna be attending the

Once you've narrowed your search down, done a walkthrough, and you've really chosen

And as you're going through these items, just make sure that when you get to the
contract stage you're gonna be asking to include these items in the contract. Including
them in the contract can really cut down on those last minute expenses. And sometimes
if you contract them earlier with the hotel it can help with cutting back on anything that
they see as a last minute, almost like a rush fee that you may have to pay for. So
anything you plan for an event in advance is really gonna be helpful for the event.
The food and beverage minimum and the room block are gonna be two things that are
really gonna work hand in hand for you when it comes to finalizing those last minute

want something, you ask for it. And even if you know that the answer may be no, at

For your food and beverage minimum, you're gonna wanna try and get as close to or
lower than your expected food budget. A lot of times you can pre-ask for the menus

any snack, tea, coffee, items like that that we're gonna go through later on, this is gonna
be something you'll wanna include into that food and beverage minimum.
The room block is a really great option to provide a discounted rate at the event location
to your attendees. But just keep in mind that the amount of rooms you're putting on
hold, we typically don't hold for the full amount of attendees unless we're 110% sure
that every single one of them will book. Because this is a contracted item, typically you
are responsible for taking on in the contract a certain amount of rooms that may or may
not book. And you're gonna wanna look at a no-show percentage in the contract called
attrition, and just see what that item is and make sure that that can be adjusted if you
need to. But once it's set, and once the contract is signed you are responsible for those
rooms, so just keep an eye out for those items.
If you do choose to do a room block, just remember that the hotel should give you
either a discount code, or a link that you can relay and send over to your attendees. This
will be the easiest way for them to book the room block, and then you don't have to try
and track them down later, or they won't go elsewhere, and hopefully they'll fill your
room block. A quick tip just about your room block, after the event is over if perhaps
you haven't filled that entire block, you can ask the hotel for a room list over the event
time from the hotel. Sometimes you'll find attendees have booked a room outside of
your block, and you can actually transfer them over into your block as a confirmation.
And hopefully filling up to that attrition point so that you don't have to pay an
additional.
The room set up is gonna be another item that you'll wanna keep in mind when going
over the final logistics with the event. You'll wanna be sure that the venue is able to set
up the necessary tables and chairs that you need to make your event exactly as you
envision it. Typically we suggest a U-shape table set with full length linens on all of the
tables. You'll want one to two tables at the front for any speakers, for the expert, and
then also one or two tables at the back of the room for staff and logistics. Any beverage
or snack tables located in the room are typically placed on the side walls closer to the
back. This is just to avoid any distraction of the event if someone does come up and
need to get a drink of water or a beverage or snack during the event, we can continue
with that item without having people looking over.
The last item on here is signage. Most venues if asked, will place a small directional sign
in the lobby or entrance telling your attendees where to find the event. Of course you
can always provide additional help, but I'll go over that a little bit later. Again, if you ask
for a lot of things from the hotel, it's really not gonna hurt anything, asking is not gonna
make them say no, but if you can get that end result that you'd like it'll make it worth it.
Once you've chosen the place that you would like to contract with, keep in mind any
